steps.dialogue: [event:6973d495c85419ee]We have two different fractions: one fraction is [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction], the other is [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction]. Take a look at the[event:69826d310086addc] shading, do you think one bar shows more, or are they the same amount?

prompt.text: Do the bars show the same amount?

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Not quite. Look at how much pink shading each bar has. Do they cover the same amount?

metadata.description: Highlight the shaded portions of both bars to show they align

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's look more closely. The[event:69792bc8f25e3ff9] top bar has 1 part shaded out of 3. The bottom bar has 2 parts shaded out of 6. Do they cover the same amount of the bar?

metadata.description: Draw alignment lines showing the shaded portions cover the same area

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let me show you. Look at the shaded areas.[event:69792e2bc758e529] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction] of the top bar and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] of the bottom bar cover the exact same amount. They're equal!

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. We can tell these [event:69791fbb936c3c46]fractions represent the same amount because the shaded portions of the bars line up.

steps.dialogue: But bars only show us one way to prove they're equivalent. Let me show you another way: using position on a number line.
[event:697935a05eb3a3f1]This time, we'll use a number line to check if fractions are equal.

[event:697939d120360113][fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]One third[/fraction] is exactly one interval from 0 toward 1. Now you try. Where do you think [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] will land on the bottom number line?[event:698272cebabfe6d6]

prompt.text: Drag a point to [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] on the bottom number line.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's try again. These fractions are equivalent. They should land at the same point.

metadata.description: Both tick marks pulse to show they should align

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]You're working on it. These fractions are [event:697a62cc7ea6acd1]equivalent, so [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction][event:698274075871738f] go at the same spot.

metadata.description: Animate counting with arrows showing 1/6, then 2/6 landing at same position as 1/3

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's work through this together.[event:697a67b61974e9c6] Starting at zero, [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction] goes to[event:698274baebf320bc] this position. For [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction], count: [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction], then[event:697a680bc4054dcc] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] lands at the [event:698276644191bd11]same spot as [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Yes, You got it! [event:697a35640f7bd429][fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]Two sixths[/fraction] lands at the exact same position as [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: [event:697a6125fedffb99]Both fractions land at the[event:697a6933b74dac8e] exact same position!

We just practiced proving equivalent fractions in two different ways:[event:697a6a3dc69b59a1] the same amount shaded in bars, AND the [event:697a6ab8392ae323]same position on the number line.[event:697a6b364f095d96] The new concept is that position proves equivalence too!
